title: Algebraic Data Types: Modeling Closed Worlds
overview: Algebraic Data Types, often abbreviated in dark-matter references as Algebraic Data Types (ADTs), combine product types and sum types to represent domain structure directly in code. They let a language describe both what data contains and which variants are valid, making impossible states harder to express by accident.
historical_context: The idea behind algebraic data types matured in the ML family, where language designers wanted a cleaner way to model symbolic structures than piles of tagged records, raw unions, or convention-heavy class hierarchies. Rather than treat data modeling as an afterthought, ML made type construction central: products bundle fields together, while sums enumerate a closed set of alternatives. Haskell deepened the style by making ADTs the ordinary way to define trees, commands, expression languages, and protocol states. This history matters because ADTs reframed type systems as tools for modeling meaning, not just storage layout. Rust later translated the same impulse into enums with payloads, bringing ADT-style design to systems programming and mainstream API work. Today the phrase Algebraic Data Types (ADTs) often appears in prose when people discuss safer backend schemas, message protocols, compiler ASTs, and typed frontend state machines, even if the language uses different surface syntax.
key_aspects: **Products and sums:** Product types collect fields that exist together, while sum types enumerate mutually exclusive variants.
**Closed case analysis:** ADTs pair naturally with pattern matching so code can handle every valid variant explicitly.
**Domain modeling:** They encode protocol states, syntax trees, result types, and command structures in a way that mirrors the problem domain.
**Modern reinterpretations:** Haskell data declarations, ML datatypes, Rust enums, and many typed API designs all express the same core idea.
technical_implications: ADTs improve program design because they compress both structure and intent into the type definition itself. A `Result` type distinguishes success from failure without relying on nulls or sentinel values. A syntax tree can preserve exactly which forms of expression are legal. Combined with pattern matching, ADTs support exhaustive handling, which means the compiler can force updates when new cases are added. This changes maintenance economics: refactors become guided by compiler feedback instead of grep and hope. In API design, ADTs encourage developers to model workflows as explicit states and transitions, which reduces ambiguity at boundaries between services, UI layers, and persistence code.
legacy: The legacy of ADTs is larger than the term itself. Even teams that do not use ML-family languages increasingly adopt ADT-style thinking through Rust enums, Swift enums with associated values, TypeScript discriminated unions, and schema-first API modeling. The concept also shaped how compilers, theorem provers, and typed UI architectures are taught. By making data design precise and finite where possible, algebraic data types helped turn the type system into a medium for architecture, not just error detection. They remain one of the clearest bridges between language theory and everyday product code.
ai_assisted_discovery_missions: 1. "Trace how Algebraic Data Types moved from ML and Haskell into Rust enums, Swift enums, and TypeScript discriminated unions."
2. "Explain how product types, sum types, and pattern matching work together when modeling a compiler AST or a network protocol."
3. "Compare class hierarchies with Algebraic Data Types for representing business workflows, including refactor safety and exhaustive handling."
